bash.text:
Execute a bash command and return its output. Commands execute in the agent's working directory.

WHEN TO USE A DEDICATED TOOL INSTEAD: prefer the dedicated Read/Grep/Glob tools over bash cat/find/grep/ls — they return structured, token-efficient results and integrate with the framework's truncation. Decision: reading a file -> Read; searching contents -> Grep; finding paths by name -> Glob. Use bash only when no dedicated tool fits (running scripts, piped commands, git, build steps, system introspection).

DESTRUCTIVE COMMANDS (rm, mv, chmod, git push, git reset --hard, drop): state your intent in the description field before calling — these are hard to reverse.

OUTPUT HANDLING: output over 30,000 chars is saved to .bash_outputs/{ts}_{hash}.log with a ~2KB inline preview. The path is in the result — page through the full output with read_text_file (offset/limit) rather than re-running the command through head/tail, which wastes a full execution.

ON TIMEOUT: do not blindly retry the identical command. Split it into smaller steps, narrow the input, or raise the timeout — then say which you chose.

grep.text:
Search file CONTENTS for regex patterns, built on ripgrep. Supports file-type filtering, context lines, case-insensitive mode, and multiple output formats. Prefer this over bash grep.

WHEN TO USE: searching inside files for a pattern. Use Glob first when you only need to find file PATHS by name — Glob is cheaper and scoped. Combine them: Glob to narrow candidate files, then Grep within those files.

OUTPUT MODE — pick by what you need from the match:
- files_with_matches (default): just the file list — cheapest, use when you only need to know WHICH files match.
- content: the matching lines (+line numbers) — use when you need to see the actual matched text or surrounding context.
- count: match counts per file — use for 'how many occurrences'.
Add context/before_context/after_context with content mode to see neighboring lines (e.g. a function signature around a call site).

REGEX NOTES: ripgrep is regex, not literal — escape literal braces (\[), parens (\(), dots (\.) and other metacharacters when searching for them verbatim, or you'll match far more than intended.

ON NO MATCHES: the pattern or scope is likely too narrow or misspelled. Broaden the glob, relax the regex, or check the path — do not assume the target is absent.

glob.text:
Fast file-PATH matching by glob pattern (e.g. '**/*.py', 'src/**/*.ts'). Returns matching paths sorted by modification time. Prefer this over bash find/ls.

WHEN TO USE: locating files by NAME or extension. Glob finds PATHS, Grep finds CONTENTS — use Glob to answer 'which files' and Grep to answer 'which files contain X'. Run Glob BEFORE Grep to scope candidate files cheaply, then Grep within them — this avoids recursively scanning an entire repo.

ON NO MATCHES: the pattern is likely too narrow. Try a broader glob ( '**' instead of '*'), drop an extension constraint, or check the base path.

sub_agent.text:
Delegate a focused, well-scoped task to an independent sub-agent that runs with its own context window and returns a result string. The sub-agent keeps the parent's context clean and lets you fan out work the parent does not need to reason over.

WHEN TO DELEGATE (vs do it yourself): delegate when the sub-task is self-contained, has a clear definition of done, and you only need its conclusion — not its scratchwork. Good fits: a broad search across many files where you want the answer not the file dumps, an isolated implementation you'll review as a whole, a verification pass. Bad fits: anything requiring tight back-and-forth with your current reasoning, or a single lookup you already know the file for.

PARALLELISM: to run several sub-agents concurrently, issue all their calls in ONE response. Calls in separate responses run sequentially. Parallel is ideal when the sub-tasks are independent (e.g. scouting different parts of the codebase).

ISOLATION: set isolation='worktree' when sub-agents will MODIFY files (edit/write code) — each runs in its own git worktree so concurrent agents don't clobber each other. Use isolation='none' (default) for read-only sub-tasks (search, analysis) where isolation is pure overhead.

CONSTRAINTS: sub-agents CANNOT spawn further sub-agents (depth capped). They run with the standard tool set minus sub_agent itself. The return value is whatever the sub-agent's final_result produced, as a string — parse it if you need structured data.

TodoTool.text:
Plan and track your task in OUTCOME-level steps, not micro-actions. You MUST call op='create' BEFORE starting any work, even for single-step tasks. Mark a step in_progress when you BEGIN it; mark it completed/skipped when the OUTCOME is done. Do NOT call update mid-step just to log progress — only at stage boundaries. Use op='list' to view current steps.

FINISHING — two distinct paths, do not mix them up:
- op='complete_remaining' (status='completed'|'skipped', reason=...): bulk-close the rest of the plan when the GOAL is already achieved early. Use this when the remaining steps are no longer necessary because the outcome is settled — status='completed' for steps the work implicitly covered, 'skipped' for steps that turned out to be unneeded. ONE call finishes everything.
- op='update' (task_id=..., status='completed'|'skipped'): close a SINGLE step at its natural stage boundary.

WHEN THE PLAN ITSELF IS WRONG — op='replace' discards the current plan entirely and starts fresh with a new items list. Use this (not complete_remaining) when you discover the original approach was misguided: the remaining steps describe work that should NOT be done, but new different work should. replace = pivot; complete_remaining = wrap up.

GATE: ALL steps MUST be terminal (completed or skipped) before the agent can finish — the framework rejects your final output otherwise, prompting you to close the open steps.
Granularity — GOOD (outcome-level): 'Fix auth bug in login flow', 'Add retry tests', 'Refactor session module'. BAD (too granular, fold into the outcome): 'Read auth.py', 'Edit line 42', 'Run pytest'.

ask_user.text:
Ask the user a question and wait for their response. SUPPORTS THREE MODES — pick the one that fits:
1. Multiple-choice: set options=[{label, description?, value?}, ...]. Use multi_select=True for checkbox (pick several). Default multi_select=False for radio (pick one).
2. Open-ended: omit options. The user types a free-form answer. Set input_type='textarea' for long answers, 'number' for numeric, 'url' for URLs.
3. Choice + other: set options AND allow_custom_input=True (default). The user can pick options AND type extra text.

WHEN TO ASK (vs decide yourself): ask only when you are genuinely blocked on a decision the user must own — a choice with no sensible default, a conflict between equally-valid options, or missing info you cannot infer. If a reasonable default exists, pick it and state why rather than interrupting. Each ask stalls the whole run, so batch related questions and avoid asking things you can answer by reading the code or config.

ALWAYS set header (short label like 'Model' or 'Priority') when using options. ALWAYS provide 2-6 options with concise labels (<=30 chars). Add description per option when the label alone is ambiguous.

FORMATTING: The question text is rendered as Markdown. Use markdown tables for comparisons, bullet lists for multiple items, and headers to separate sections. NEVER dump a long unformatted paragraph — structure the question so it's easy to scan at a glance.

Returns the user's answer as a plain string. Blocks until answered (default waits forever; configurable via HARNESS_ASK_USER_TIMEOUT env).

render_chart.text:
Render a chart visualization (bar, line, scatter, heatmap, etc.) to the frontend. Use this to visualize data analysis results. The chart will appear inline in the conversation AND in the Results tab.

CHART_TYPE BY DATA SHAPE — match the chart to what the data expresses:
- line: a continuous series over an ordered axis (time, epoch, step).
- bar: comparing a metric across discrete categories.
- scatter: correlation between two numeric variables (one point per row).
- heatmap: density / intensity across two categorical axes.
- box: distribution summary per group (quartiles + outliers).
- bubble: three-variable scatter (x, y, and a size dimension).
- area: cumulative or part-to-whole over an ordered axis.
- radar: multi-axis profile comparison.
- pareto: ranked bars + cumulative line (80/20 analysis).
- waterfall: sequential contribution to a running total.
- table: when precise values matter more than shape.
Use x/y (and hue for grouping) to map columns. Pick the simplest type that conveys the point — a table beats a cluttered chart for exact numbers.

Args:
  data: List of row dicts (e.g. [{'month':'Jan','sales':100}, ...]).
  chart_type: see mapping above.
  x: X-axis column name.
  y: Y-axis column name.
  label: Group label for collapsible sections (default 'default').
  title: Chart title.
  hue: Color-grouping column.
  size: Bubble size column (chart_type='bubble' only).
  series: Series definitions for dist_overlay. Each entry: {'key': col_name, 'type': 'area'|'line', 'axis': 'left'|'right', 'color': '#hex', 'fillOpacity': 0.2, 'dash': '6 3', 'step': false, 'label': 'display name', 'strokeWidth': 1.5}.
